first , the conventional method functions best when we base the [x0] expansion on a flat hypersurface immediately after horizon exit . in our method , we make the opposite choice and move the flat hypersurface as close as possible to the time of observation . after this , the role of the [x0] formula is to provide no more than the non - linear gauge transformation between field fluctuations and the curvature perturbation .

our new method joins many well - established techniques for estimating non - gaussian properties of the curvature perturbation . in our experience , these techniques give comparable estimates of [x1] , but they do not exactly agree .

each method invokes different assumptions , such as the neglect of gradients or the degree to which time dependence can be accommodated .

the mutual scatter between different methods can be attributed to the theory error inherent in any estimate of [x1] .

gaussianity . in comparison , our proposed moment transport method exhibits several clear differences .

first , the conventional method functions best when we base the [x0] expansion on a flat hypersurface immediately after horizon exit . in our method , we make the opposite choice and move the flat hypersurface as close as possible to the time of observation . after this , the role of the [x0] formula is to provide no more than the non - linear gauge transformation between field fluctuations and the curvature perturbation .

than the non - linear gauge transformation between field fluctuations and the curvature perturbation .

we substitute the method of moment transport to evolve the distribution of field fluctuations between horizon exit and observation .

second , in integrating the transport equation one uses an expansion of the velocity field such as the one given in eqs .

. this expansion is refreshed at each step of integration , so the result is related to conventional perturbative calculations in a very similar way to renormalization - group improved perturbation theory [ref] . in this interpretation ,

in time evolution , but may be an advantage in comparison with perturbative methods .

we also note that although [x0] is non - perturbative as a point of principle , practical implementations are frequently perturbative .

for example , the method of vernizzi & wands [ref] and battefeld & easther [ref] depends on the existence of quantities which are conserved only to leading order in [x178] , and can lose accuracy after [x179] e - foldings .

methods can be attributed to the theory error inherent in any estimate of [x1] .

the comparison presented in

[ sec : numerics ] shows that while all of these methods slightly disagree , the moment transport method gives good agreement with other established methods .

inflation generically predicts a primordial spectrum of density perturbations which is almost precisely gaussian [ref] . in recent years

the small non - gaussian component [ref] has emerged as an important observable [ref] , and will be measured with good precision by the _

planck surveyor _ satellite [ref] . in the near future ,

but only for very specific choices of the potential [ref] or hubble rate [ref] .

these formulas become increasingly cumbersome as the number of fields increases , or if one studies higher moments [ref] . in the future , it seems clear that studies of complex models with many fields will increasingly rely on numerical methods .

the numerical [x0] framework requires the solution to a number of ordinary differential equations which scales exponentially with the number of fields .

since some models include hundreds of fields , this may present a significant obstacle [ref] .

has been used by lehners & renaux - petel to obtain numerical results [ref] .

another numerical scheme has been introduced by huston & malik [ref] .

what properties do we require of a successful prediction ? consider a typical observer , drawn at random from an ensemble of realizations of inflation . in any of the formalisms

discussed above , we aim to estimate the statistical properties of the curvature perturbation which would be measured by such an observer .
